title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a reserve officer called Simon talking about volunteer opportunities at a bird reserve. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a reserve officer called Simon talking about volunteer opportunities at a bird reserve. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A reserve officer called Simon talks about volunteer opportunities at a bird reserve.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Simon, visitor officer at Marshland Bird Reserve, an area of lakes and wet grassland managed for wildlife and open to the public. Visitors are often surprised that this peaceful landscape was not always natural-looking. One of our largest lakes occupies the site of an old [9]brick quarry[/9], which filled with water after digging ended. Over time, birds began visiting the area, and conservation organisations worked with local residents to protect it. We now welcome school trips, walkers, photographers and regular volunteers.||Birds can be seen throughout the year, but different seasons bring different visitors. People often ask about swans and ducks because they are easy to notice. However, the bird creating most excitement this spring has been the [10]kingfisher[/10], after several visitors saw its bright colour near a small river channel. Volunteers attend an induction before starting. While learning where facilities are is helpful, the most important rule is maintaining a [11]quiet distance[/11] from wildlife, particularly near nests or feeding areas. Binoculars allow people to observe without getting too close.||New helpers have varied jobs. Some welcome visitors, while others assist reserve staff outdoors. Most beginners first take responsibility for checking [12]information signs[/12] along the paths, recording any that are damaged, unclear or covered by plants. This allows them to become familiar with the reserve safely. Volunteers working outside should take waterproof clothing, and because paths can be uneven and wet, we particularly recommend [13]walking boots[/13]. We provide high-visibility jackets when teams need to work near visitor routes or car parks.||An important part of our work is collecting data. Birds are observed from sheltered structures rather than followed through habitats. This summer, experienced volunteers will help count birds from the new [14]viewing hide[/14] beside the reed beds. We also want to involve visitors in understanding wetland life. Our school programme includes pond activities, and the event which teachers have booked most often this year is a [15]minibeast survey[/15], where children carefully observe small creatures and return them safely to their habitat. All equipment is cleaned between school groups.||Volunteers attending twelve sessions may join a free [16]bird identification[/16] course, led by a specialist over two mornings. It is designed for interested beginners rather than experts. At the end of summer, volunteers and local families will be invited to a [17]sunset walk[/17], when reserve staff explain evening sounds and animal activity. The event will collect donations for our next improvement. At present, our outdoor paths are difficult for visitors with reduced mobility after heavy rain. Money raised will help create a new [18]boardwalk[/18] through the wettest section, improving access while protecting the habitat below. Information about volunteering is available in the visitor centre.

dialogue.speaker: simon
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Simon, visitor officer at Marshland Bird Reserve, an area of lakes and wet grassland managed for wildlife and open to the public. Visitors are often surprised that this peaceful landscape was not always natural-looking. One of our largest lakes occupies the site of an old brick quarry, which filled with water after digging ended. Over time, birds began visiting the area, and conservation organisations worked with local residents to protect it. We now welcome school trips, walkers, photographers and regular volunteers.||Birds can be seen throughout the year, but different seasons bring different visitors. People often ask about swans and ducks because they are easy to notice. However, the bird creating most excitement this spring has been the kingfisher, after several visitors saw its bright colour near a small river channel. Volunteers attend an induction before starting. While learning where facilities are is helpful, the most important rule is maintaining a quiet distance from wildlife, particularly near nests or feeding areas. Binoculars allow people to observe without getting too close.||New helpers have varied jobs. Some welcome visitors, while others assist reserve staff outdoors. Most beginners first take responsibility for checking information signs along the paths, recording any that are damaged, unclear or covered by plants. This allows them to become familiar with the reserve safely. Volunteers working outside should take waterproof clothing, and because paths can be uneven and wet, we particularly recommend walking boots. We provide high-visibility jackets when teams need to work near visitor routes or car parks.||An important part of our work is collecting data. Birds are observed from sheltered structures rather than followed through habitats. This summer, experienced volunteers will help count birds from the new viewing hide beside the reed beds. We also want to involve visitors in understanding wetland life. Our school programme includes pond activities, and the event which teachers have booked most often this year is a minibeast survey, where children carefully observe small creatures and return them safely to their habitat. All equipment is cleaned between school groups.||Volunteers attending twelve sessions may join a free bird identification course, led by a specialist over two mornings. It is designed for interested beginners rather than experts. At the end of summer, volunteers and local families will be invited to a sunset walk, when reserve staff explain evening sounds and animal activity. The event will collect donations for our next improvement. At present, our outdoor paths are difficult for visitors with reduced mobility after heavy rain. Money raised will help create a new boardwalk through the wettest section, improving access while protecting the habitat below. Information about volunteering is available in the visitor centre.

questions.question: One of the reserve lakes was formerly a (9) .
questions.explanation: Simon says the lake occupies the site of an old 'brick quarry'.

questions.question: The bird attracting most interest this spring is the (10) .
questions.explanation: Simon identifies the 'kingfisher' as creating most excitement.

questions.explanation: Simon states that volunteers must maintain a 'quiet distance'.

questions.explanation: Simon says new helpers begin by checking 'information signs'.

questions.question: Simon strongly advises outdoor volunteers to wear (13) .
Experienced volunteers will count birds from the new (14) .

questions.explanation: Simon identifies the 'minibeast survey' as most booked.

Families and volunteers will be invited to a (17) .

questions.explanation: Simon says money will create a new 'boardwalk'.
